title: Split DIV/REM off fast path
motivation:
The current ALU keeps 32-bit DIV/REM in the same combinational path as the
fast integer ops. That is wasted timing budget on CoreMark, which is much
more branch/load/multiply heavy than divide heavy.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current ALU keeps 32-bit DIV/REM in the same combinational path as the
fast integer ops. That is wasted timing budget on CoreMark, which is much
more branch/load/multiply heavy than divide heavy.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep ADD/SUB/logic/shifts/multiply single-cycle, but move only DIV/DIVU/REM/
REMU into a small iterative side unit that asserts busy until the quotient or
remainder is ready. Freeze younger stages through the existing stall path
while a divide runs, preserve the ALTOPS/formal stand-ins, and keep the RV32M
corner-case results exact. The common path should synthesize faster without
changing CoreMark-visible behavior.

motivation:

CoreMark is branch-heavy, so moving control-flow resolution earlier should
cut wrong-path fetches and shorten the critical EX redirect path.

title: Tiny BTB/BHT predictor
motivation:
CoreMark spends much of its time in hot loops and repeated call/return
paths, but the current front end always fetches pc+4 and only redirects
after EX resolves the control transfer. That means every taken branch
still burns fetch bandwidth and injects bubbles.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark spends much of its time in hot loops and repeated call/return
paths, but the current front end always fetches pc+4 and only redirects
after EX resolves the control transfer. That means every taken branch
still burns fetch bandwidth and injects bubbles.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 16-entry direct-mapped BTB/BHT in IF, trained from EX on resolved
control transfers, and carry the predicted next PC through the pipeline
so correct predictions skip the redirect bubble. CoreMark's repeated
loop branches and JAL/JALR targets should hit often enough to improve
iter/cycle more than the tiny table costs in LUTs.

title: Bypass load-use bubbles
motivation:

or address use. The current core always pays a one-cycle load-use bubble, even
on the zero-wait FPGA path, so that hot pattern leaves easy CPI on the table.

suppresses redirects until the load data is really available.

title: Two-entry fetch skid buffer
motivation:
The current front end couples instruction delivery directly to decode, so
bus stalls and decode backpressure both hit the same critical path. A tiny
registered fetch buffer can decouple refilling from consumption, which should
improve Fmax and hide short front-end bubbles without changing in-order retire
behavior.

hypothesis.hypothesis:

fetch backpressure.

title: Opcode-gated source pruning
motivation:
The current hazard and bypass logic treats the raw rs1/rs2 bitfields as
live for every opcode, even when the instruction is immediate-only, a
jump, or another form that ignores one or both source registers. That
can create false load-use stalls when dead bits alias a pending rd and
it keeps extra compare fanout on the common path.

CoreMark mixes loads with immediate arithmetic, jumps, and other opcodes
that do not read both source fields. Today the load-use detector compares
raw rs bitfields anyway, so dead instruction bits can alias a pending load
destination and create avoidable bubbles.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark mixes loads with immediate arithmetic, jumps, and other opcodes
that do not read both source fields. Today the load-use detector compares
raw rs bitfields anyway, so dead instruction bits can alias a pending load
destination and create avoidable bubbles.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Predecode rs1/rs2 usage once in IF, carry those two live-source bits with
the fetched bundle, and use them only to qualify the load-use compare in
hazard_unit. That should eliminate dead-field alias stalls while keeping the
forwarding network and RVFI path unchanged, so the fix stays narrow enough
to help CPI without widening the critical EX cone.

so the next likely bottleneck is the fetch-to-decode cone that every
instruction still pays. A correctly killable IF/ID register should shorten
that path without introducing new speculation state.

motivation:

shared return-address stack targets the remaining JALR misses without
bringing back BTB/BHT-style tag state.

zero-wait FPGA path, so those common sequences still waste cycles.
Reclaiming that CPI without adding new speculation state is the cleanest
remaining micro-opt lever.

title: Link-shadow return predictor
motivation:
CoreMark still leaves a few bubbles on canonical JALR returns after the
shared BTFNT/JAL predictor, but the previous return fast paths all ran into
stale targets or hidden history. A tiny x1/x5 shadow that tracks the newest
in-flight link-register write should let fetch predict returns from the right
value without adding a stack or BTB state.

value without adding a stack or BTB state.

other control flow stays on the existing BTFNT/JAL path. This should recover
the remaining call/return bubbles in CoreMark without introducing hidden
predictor history.

title: Direct load bypass
motivation:
CoreMark still spends a lot of time in load-to-use chains, especially in
word loads feeding address arithmetic and checksum updates. The current
core always injects a fixed one-cycle bubble even when the memory path is
zero-wait, so those common sequences leave easy IPC on the table.

Add a direct MEM-stage load bypass so the instruction behind a load can
consume `dmem_rdata` in EX on the very next cycle when `io_dmemReady` is
high. Keep the existing stall/replay path for real backpressure, and make
the bypassed operand snapshot feed RVFI through the same EX/MEM/WB path so
retired values still match what actually executed. This targets the hottest
CoreMark dependency pattern without adding any new speculation state.

title: Forwarded operand snapshot
motivation:
The bypass compare tree still sits on the hot EX operand path even though the
dependency choice is known one stage earlier. The earlier select-latch
attempts showed the snapshot has to stay aligned with the live producer
state, so capturing the resolved operand values in ID should remove the EX
compare/mux cone without changing architectural behavior.

dependency choice is known one stage earlier. The earlier select-latch
attempts showed the snapshot has to stay aligned with the live producer
state, so capturing the resolved operand values in ID should remove the EX
compare/mux cone without changing architectural behavior.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move the forwarding decision into ID and latch the resolved rs1/rs2 operand
values, not just the select bits, as part of the ID/EX bundle. Refresh that
operand snapshot whenever the instruction is held so the values stay aligned
with the current EX/MEM and MEM/WB producers, then let EX consume the latched
operands directly. This keeps the hot execute cone free of the bypass compare
tree while preserving the existing stall/flush semantics and RVFI-visible
